Name: ross-style-business-rules
Description: A small, complete claims model demonstrating declarative business rules rendering themselves as RuleSpeak and tracing down to ground truth. Five rules incorporate Ronald Ross's corrections: (1) policy 'active' is ground truth, not a derived system rule; (2) high-risk uses 'must be considered ... if', not 'when'; (3) claimant of record is a priority ordering, never 'otherwise/else'; (4) valid claim is a conjunction of declarative conditions; (5) approvable is 'only if valid' (necessary, not sufficient) — not 'iff'.

Policies.Description: A coverage agreement. Whether a policy is active is a fact about the policy (ground truth), recorded by the system — not a derived rule.

schema.name: Name
schema.Description: Logical primary key — mirrors PolicyId so every row is addressable by Name.

schema.name: PolicyId
schema.Description: Stable identifier for the policy.

schema.name: PolicyholderName
schema.Description: Name of the policyholder.

schema.name: IsActive
schema.Description: Ground truth: whether the policy is currently in force. Per R. Ross: being active is a business STATE — a fact about the policy — not a derived business rule. The system's 'Is Active' indicator merely records this fact, so it lives at the leaves, not in a rule.

schema.name: CountOfCoveredClaimants
schema.Description: Number of claimants whose current policy is this one.

Claimants.Description: A person linked to incidents and (optionally) to claims. Carries a current policy, a favorite color, and a derived incident count.

schema.name: Name
schema.Description: Logical primary key — mirrors ClaimantName so every row is addressable by Name.

schema.name: ClaimantId
schema.Description: Stable identifier for the claimant.

schema.name: ClaimantName
schema.Description: Display name of the claimant.

schema.name: FavoriteColor
schema.Description: The claimant's favorite color (a single-select attribute).

schema.name: Policy
schema.Description: The claimant's current policy (foreign key to Policies).

schema.name: CountOfIncidents
schema.Description: The number of incidents whose claimant is this claimant.

schema.name: IsHighRisk
schema.Description: A claimant must be considered high-risk if the claimant's incident count exceeds 2. (Ross: 'must be considered ... if'; '>2' means 3 or more.)

schema.name: PolicyIsActive
schema.Description: Whether the claimant's current policy is active (ground truth carried over from the policy).

Incidents.Description: An event or loss that can give rise to a claim. Each incident involves exactly one claimant of record at the incident level.

schema.name: Name
schema.Description: Logical primary key — mirrors IncidentId so every row is addressable by Name.

schema.name: IncidentId
schema.Description: Stable identifier for the incident.

schema.name: IncidentDescription
schema.Description: Short description of the incident.

schema.name: Claimant
schema.Description: The claimant involved in this incident (foreign key to Claimants via ClaimantId).

schema.name: IncidentClaimantName
schema.Description: Name of the incident's claimant (for readability).

Claims.Description: A request to have an incident evaluated under a policy. Validity and approvability are computed, never entered.

schema.name: Name
schema.Description: Logical primary key — mirrors ClaimId so every row is addressable by Name.

schema.name: ClaimId
schema.Description: Stable identifier for the claim.

schema.name: Incident
schema.Description: The incident this claim references (exactly one; foreign key to Incidents via IncidentId).

schema.name: AdditionalClaimant
schema.Description: An optional second claimant linked to the same claim (foreign key to Claimants via ClaimantId).

schema.name: IsFlaggedForReview
schema.Description: Ground truth: whether the claim has been flagged for manual review (e.g., possible fraud). A claim can be valid yet still be held here — which is exactly why 'approvable' is 'only if valid', not 'iff valid'.

schema.name: ReferencesIncident
schema.Description: A claim references an incident when its incident is present.

schema.name: HasAdditionalClaimant
schema.Description: A claim has an additional claimant when one is present.

schema.name: IncidentClaimant
schema.Description: The claimant of the referenced incident.

schema.name: ClaimantOfRecord
schema.Description: The claimant of record for a claim is determined by priority: (1) the additional claimant, if one exists; (2) otherwise the incident's claimant. Per R. Ross: expressed as a priority ordering, not an 'otherwise/else' procedure — the ordering is the rule.

schema.name: IncidentClaimantPolicyActive
schema.Description: Whether the incident claimant's current policy is active.

schema.name: AdditionalClaimantPolicyActive
schema.Description: Whether the additional claimant's current policy is active (false when there is no additional claimant).

schema.name: AdditionalClaimantFavoriteColor
schema.Description: The additional claimant's favorite color (blank when there is no additional claimant).

schema.name: ClaimantOfRecordIncidentCount
schema.Description: The incident count of the claimant of record.

schema.name: ClaimantOfRecordIsHighRisk
schema.Description: Whether the claimant of record is high-risk (DR-2). Carries the named rule — IsValid references this field rather than re-deriving the threshold inline, keeping the '> 2 incidents' definition in exactly one place.

schema.name: IsValid
schema.Description: All of the following must be true for a claim to be considered valid: it references an incident; at least one of the incident claimant's or the additional claimant's current policy is active; if an additional claimant exists, that claimant's favorite color is red; and the claimant of record is not high-risk (DR-2). Note: DR-4's fourth condition intentionally references the named high-risk rule rather than restating the incident-count threshold.

schema.name: ValidityDecidingFactor
schema.Description: Names the single deciding reason for a claim's validity verdict — the first unmet condition, or that all conditions are met.
schema.formula: =IF({{IsValid}}, "Valid — all conditions met", IF(NOT({{ReferencesIncident}}), "No incident referenced", IF(NOT(OR({{IncidentClaimantPolicyActive}}, {{AdditionalClaimantPolicyActive}})), "No active policy on incident claimant or additional claimant", IF(AND({{HasAdditionalClaimant}}, LOWER({{AdditionalClaimantFavoriteColor}}) <> "red"), "Additional claimant's favorite color is not red", IF({{ClaimantOfRecordIsHighRisk}}, "Claimant of record is high-risk (DR-2)", "Undetermined")))))

schema.name: IsApprovable
schema.Description: A claim may be considered approvable only if it is valid and not flagged for review. Per R. Ross: 'only if', not 'if and only if' — validity is necessary but not sufficient. Stating it as 'iff' would wrongly make 'valid' and 'approvable' the same concept.

schema.name: ApprovabilityDecidingFactor
schema.Description: Names the single deciding reason for a claim's approvability verdict.
schema.formula: =IF({{IsApprovable}}, "Approvable", IF(NOT({{IsValid}}), "Not approvable — claim is not valid", IF({{IsFlaggedForReview}}, "Valid, but flagged for review", "Undetermined")))

data.Name: claim-B
data.ApprovabilityDecidingFactor: Not approvable — claim is not valid

data.Name: claim-C
data.ValidityDecidingFactor: Additional claimant's favorite color is not red
data.ApprovabilityDecidingFactor: Not approvable — claim is not valid

__meta__.Description: Project-level metadata that travels with the rulebook: tagline, motif, narrative descriptions, substrate list, signature rows, etc. One row per metadata key. Use ValueType to interpret StringValue vs JsonValue.

schema.name: MetaKey
schema.Description: The metadata key (e.g. 'tagline', 'motif_palette', 'substrates'). Unique within the table.

schema.name: Name
schema.Description: Identifier for this metadata entry. Mirrors MetaKey so the row is addressable by Name like every other table.

schema.name: ValueType
schema.Description: How to interpret the value columns: 'string' (use StringValue), 'object' (parse JsonValue as JSON object), 'array' (parse JsonValue as JSON array).

schema.name: StringValue
schema.Description: Plain string value. Populated when ValueType == 'string'; null otherwise.

schema.name: JsonValue
schema.Description: JSON-encoded value. Populated when ValueType == 'object' or 'array'; null when ValueType == 'string'.

data.Name: tagline
data.StringValue: Five declarative rules, four entities, one dataset that proves 'valid' and 'approvable' are not the same concept.

data.Name: description_rich
data.StringValue: A small, complete claims model built to demonstrate Ronald Ross's five corrections to business-rule wording. Every verdict (IsValid, IsApprovable) is computed from raw leaves only — IsActive, FavoriteColor, the incident→claimant links, and the review flag — and each verdict names its single deciding factor. The dataset exercises every branch: claim-D is valid yet not approvable (proving the 'only if' vs 'iff' distinction); claim-C fails on color; claim-B fails on high-risk; claim-E passes via the additional claimant's active policy.

data.Name: ross_corrections
data.JsonValue: ["DR-1 (active policy): demoted from a derived 'system rule' to raw ground truth on Policies.IsActive.","DR-2 (high-risk): worded 'a claimant must be considered high-risk if incident count exceeds 2'.","DR-3 (claimant of record): a priority ordering (additional claimant, then incident claimant), not 'otherwise/else'.","DR-4 (valid claim): a conjunction of declarative conditions with a nested 'at least one of' disjunction.","DR-5 (approvable): 'only if valid' (and not flagged) — necessary, not sufficient; deliberately not 'iff'."]

data.Name: cmcc_summary
data.StringValue: Five declarative rules over four entities (Policies, Claimants, Incidents, Claims). Every verdict is computed from raw leaves only. Each verdict names its single deciding factor. The dataset is chosen so every condition is exercised by both a passing and a failing claim, including claim-D — the concrete proof that 'valid' and 'approvable' are not the same concept, and therefore that the rule is 'only if', not 'iff'.
